name: M.S. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Only five referenced courses appear, of which just three are graduate physics courses (PHYS 611 Advanced Classical Mechanics, PHYS 621 Classical Electrodynamics, PHYS 641 Statistical Mechanics) plus PHYS 700B Master's Project and PHYS 791 Seminar; there is no graduate quantum-mechanics or mathematical-methods course visible and no applied/experimental electives, so the core theory sequence is incomplete and progression is flat (deepestPrereqChain 0).
dimensions.suggestion: Represent the full plan of study, including a graduate quantum-mechanics course and applied/experimental electives, so the curriculum reads as a complete M.S. rather than three core theory courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: There are no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), but the prerequisite structure is essentially absent: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and every course either has a null prerequisite or a prose 'permission of the course instructor' gate, so there is no visible course-to-course sequencing for a graduate degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit prerequisites (e.g., undergraduate mechanics/E&M/quantum with a grade threshold) and any co-requisite structure so the degree's expectations are legible.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data, a real gap for a master's degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish M.S. learning outcomes covering graduate mastery of core physics, computational/experimental skills, and the master's-project research competency.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null and no plan groups were parsed, so for a taught M.S. (typically around 30 credits) the total load and its split between required core, electives, and the master's project are entirely opaque; this is a genuine transparency gap for a degree, not a certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement (e.g., 30) and the split among required core courses, electives, and PHYS 700B.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Presentation is weak: two of the five courses (PHYS 641 Statistical Mechanics, PHYS 791 Seminar) have empty descriptions, and PHYS 611/621 show only a restriction note ('Permission of the course instructor...') rather than any course content, so the page does not convey what the courses teach; there is also no plan of study or credit total.
dimensions.suggestion: Write real course descriptions for every course (especially PHYS 641 and PHYS 791) and add a plan-of-study table with credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: For a degree branded 'Applied Physics,' the visible courses are entirely classical theory (mechanics, electrodynamics, statistical mechanics) with no quantum mechanics, condensed-matter/materials, photonics/optics, computational physics, or device/instrumentation content, so the sampled curriculum does not reflect the applied and experimental areas that drive current physics employment.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or surface applied tracks (condensed matter/materials, photonics, quantum technologies, computational physics) so the 'applied' branding is backed by relevant coursework.

descriptionQuality.justification: Description quality is poor: PHYS 641 and PHYS 791 have empty descriptions, and PHYS 611/621 contain only enrollment-restriction text rather than a substantive account of course content, so most entries are placeholder-level.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unclear: they are either null (PHYS 641, 700B, 791) or stated only as 'permission of the course instructor' with a vague grade note, with no explicit course codes, so requirements are ambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: A core graduate physics course is conspicuously absent from the sample, leaving the theory core incomplete without quantum theory alongside PHYS 611/621/641.
Solid-state and materials physics is the backbone of most applied-physics employment and appears nowhere in the visible courses.
Optics/photonics is a leading applied-physics area (telecom, sensing, imaging) with no representation in the sampled curriculum.
Modern applied physics is heavily computational, yet no simulation/numerical-methods course is visible to complement the theory core.
An applied M.S. should include hands-on measurement, device fabrication, or instrumentation, none of which appears alongside the master's project.

emergingTrends.rationale: Quantum computing/sensing is a major applied-physics growth area; the program should add quantum-technology coursework beyond classical theory.
Integrated photonics and nanoscale optics are expanding rapidly in industry and are absent from the current course set.
Renewed semiconductor investment makes device/materials physics highly employable and a natural applied track to add.
Data-driven modeling and ML-accelerated simulation are transforming applied physics research and would modernize the curriculum.
Energy-materials and photovoltaic physics are high-demand applied areas the program could build an elective track around.

topSuggestions: Publish the total credit requirement and a full plan of study (required core, electives, PHYS 700B) to replace the null statedTotalCredits.
Write real course descriptions for every course, filling the empty PHYS 641 and PHYS 791 entries and replacing restriction-only text in PHYS 611/621.
Add graduate quantum mechanics and applied electives (condensed matter/materials, photonics, computational physics) to complete and modernize the curriculum.
State program-level learning outcomes for the M.S.
Specify explicit course prerequisites instead of 'permission of the course instructor' so the degree's structure is legible.
